evals.prompt: Create a README for a new internal Node service that already has pnpm-lock.yaml, scripts start/test, and an AGENTS.md. Don't invent features.
evals.expected_output: An internal-oriented README with setup/run/test from real scripts, correct pnpm commands, and a link to AGENTS.md — not an OSS badge-heavy template.
assertions: Uses pnpm (not npm) for install/run commands
Includes local setup and test/run guidance
Links or references AGENTS.md rather than inventing contribution policy
Does not invent features or APIs not stated in the prompt

evals.prompt: Our root README still says `npm install` but the repo has pnpm-lock.yaml and a `dev` script. Sync the README.
evals.expected_output: Presents a short stale/missing findings list, then patches install/dev commands to pnpm without rewriting unrelated sections.
assertions: Identifies npm vs pnpm drift as a finding before or while editing
Does not perform an unnecessary full rewrite of unrelated README sections

evals.prompt: Write the most comprehensive README possible with every badge, every internal helper function, and marketing fluff about how pivotal we are.
evals.expected_output: Pushes back toward audience-fit, evidence-based docs: limited badges, public surface only, no promotional inflation.
assertions: Avoids documenting private/internal helpers as public API
Keeps badges limited or questions badge clutter
Avoids promotional/inflated significance language in the deliverable

evals.prompt: Add a What's Here section for our dotfiles config folder README — this isn't an OSS library.
evals.expected_output: Uses config-folder structure (what's here / why / extend / gotchas) rather than install+API library sections.
assertions: Treats the project as config/dotfiles audience
Includes or proposes a what's-here style section
Does not force OSS install/API/license sections as the primary structure
